_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the AL Black Belt cluster (Dallas, Marengo, Sumter, Greene, Perry, Hale, Wilcox, Lowndes counties). Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across two eras: the plantation/enslaved labor system (1820-1865) and the sharecropping system (1865-1960). The cotton calendar dominated both eras — plant in March-April, chop May-June, pick August-November — but the human experience of that calendar was radically different for the enslaver and the enslaved, the landowner and the sharecropper. The Black Belt's calendar was cotton's calendar, and cotton's calendar was relentless.
display_name: AL Black Belt — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Cotton monoculture (plantation enslaved labor 1820-1865; sharecropping 1865-1960); subsidiary corn, livestock, and garden agriculture

calendar_eras.description: The cotton calendar on Black Belt plantations was a year-round cycle of forced labor. The enslaved workforce had no lay-by period in the way that free farmers did — enslaved people were put to other tasks (clearing land, ditching, splitting rails, repairing buildings) whenever the cotton did not demand their labor in the fields. The planter class experienced the same calendar as a cycle of profit and social ritual: cotton planting, cotton picking, cotton selling, and the winter social season. The contrast between the enslaved person's experience and the planter's experience of the same months is the fundamental reality of the Black Belt calendar.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Enslaved workers assigned to maintenance tasks: splitting rails for fences, clearing new ground, ditching for drainage, repairing buildings and equipment. On larger plantations, the work never stopped — the overseer's task system filled every day. Hog killing if not completed in December. Firewood cutting.
january.social_civic: For the planter class: the winter social season. Visiting between plantations. Balls and dinners at the larger houses. Political discussions at the county seat. For the enslaved: January was hiring-out month on some plantations — enslaved workers were hired to other planters or to Selma businesses for the year, separating families. This was one of the cruelest features of the system.
january.economic: Cotton from the fall harvest was being shipped downriver to Mobile. Planters settled accounts with cotton factors. The domestic slave trade was active in winter — slave traders bought and sold in Selma during the hiring-out season. January was when enslaved families learned whether they would be separated.
january.weather_conditions: Cool to cold — average 42-50F. Hard freezes uncommon but possible. The Black Belt winter was milder than the Piedmont upcountry — the Deep South location kept temperatures moderate. Rain was frequent; the heavy clay soil became a quagmire.
january.narrative_hook: January in the Black Belt was hiring-out month — {ancestor_name} learned whether the year would be spent at home or at a stranger's plantation, whether the family would stay together or be scattered across the county.

february.agricultural: Preparation for spring planting began. Breaking ground with mule-drawn plows on the heavy clay soil. The Black Belt's clay was brutally difficult to plow — mules labored and plows broke on the dense, sticky earth. Enslaved laborers cleared new ground: felling trees, grubbing stumps, burning brush.
february.social_civic: Planter social season continuing. Court session at the county seat. For enslaved workers, February was the beginning of the most physically demanding phase of the year.
february.economic: Planters negotiating cotton factors for the coming year's advances. Seed purchases. Equipment repair. The economic cycle was beginning again.
february.weather_conditions: Cool and wet — 45-55F. Rain frequent. The clay soil held water, making plowing miserable — both for the enslaved workers and for the mules.
february.narrative_hook: February in {county} County was when the plow met the Black Belt clay — {ancestor_name} behind the mule, fighting soil so heavy and sticky that it pulled the plow sideways and caked on boots until walking felt like wading.

march.agricultural: Cotton planting began in late March on the Black Belt prairies — earlier than in the Piedmont because of the lower latitude and milder climate. Corn planting on bottomland fields. Garden plots prepared near slave quarters. The planting window was critical: too early risked frost damage; too late shortened the growing season.
march.social_civic: Easter observances — both in the planter's church and in the enslaved community's praise house. Court session. Camp meetings began in some communities.
march.economic: Full commitment to the crop year. Planter credit extended against the coming harvest. Every resource — labor, equipment, land — was now devoted to cotton.
march.weather_conditions: Warming rapidly — 55-65F. Spring rains essential for germination but heavy rain on the clay soil delayed planting. Dogwood and redbud blooming on the hills; the prairie remained mostly treeless.
march.narrative_hook: March planting in {county} County was when the year's fate was committed to the dark soil — {ancestor_name} dropping cotton seed in furrows of black clay, knowing the harvest was eight months away and every day between was the overseer's.

april.agricultural: Cotton planting continued. Early-planted cotton germinating. Corn up in the bottomland fields. The labor demand was intense — every enslaved worker in the fields, planting, replanting where stands were poor, and beginning the first cultivation.
april.social_civic: Easter (if not in March). Church services. The planter social season winding down as the agricultural calendar consumed attention.
april.economic: Planter families fully committed to the crop. No income until the fall harvest. Credit extended at maximum.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 62-72F. Thunderstorms. The Black Belt prairie was dramatic in spring — dark soil, green cotton coming up, thunderheads building over the flat horizon.
april.narrative_hook: By April, the cotton was up and the year belonged to the crop — {ancestor_name} in the fields from first light, the overseer's watch the only clock, and eight months of labor between the seed and the gin.

may.agricultural: Cotton chopping — the first intensive weeding with hoes. Enslaved workers hoed between rows from dawn to dusk. The task system on some plantations assigned specific amounts of ground per day; failure to complete the task meant punishment. Cotton cultivation with plow and sweep. Corn growing. Garden production beginning.
may.social_civic: Virtually all social activity subordinated to fieldwork. Sunday rest was observed on most plantations but the extent varied with the overseer.
may.economic: No income. Full labor demand. The plantation was a self-contained economy devoted entirely to producing cotton.
may.weather_conditions: Hot — 72-80F. Humidity increasing. Mosquitoes and malaria risk rising in the bottomland areas. The heat was oppressive for field labor.
may.narrative_hook: May was chopping month — {ancestor_name} with a hoe in the cotton rows of {county} County from sunrise to sunset, the dark soil throwing back the heat, the overseer counting tasks and the whip enforcing the count.

june.agricultural: Second cultivation. Cotton growing. Weeding continued. Corn tasseling. The labor intensity remained extreme — the cotton demanded constant attention to maintain weed-free rows.
june.social_civic: Minimal. The heat and the fieldwork consumed everything. Sunday services at the praise house or under the brush arbor.
june.economic: No income. No respite. The enslaved worker's calendar had no vacation.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 78-86F. Thunderstorms. Malaria season beginning in the river bottoms. Yellow fever risk in some years.
june.narrative_hook: June in the Black Belt was when the heat and the cotton merged into a single oppression — {ancestor_name} in the field under a sun that baked the dark clay into concrete, the air thick with humidity, and the overseer's shadow longer than the shade of any tree the prairie offered.

july.agricultural: Lay-by for the cotton — the crop was growing on its own and required less intensive labor. But on Black Belt plantations, 'lay-by' for the enslaved rarely meant rest — overseers assigned maintenance tasks: clearing new ground, repairing fences, ditching, hauling. The idea of a break was the planter's luxury, not the enslaved worker's.
july.social_civic: Camp meetings and revival season began. Brush arbor meetings for the enslaved community. July Fourth — observed by planters as a holiday; for the enslaved, the irony was bitter. Some plantations allowed a brief holiday.
july.economic: No income. The plantation economy was in waiting mode — the crop was growing but not yet harvestable. Some planters shipped last year's remaining cotton.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 80-88F. Drought risk. Intense humidity. The Black Belt summer was among the most physically punishing in the South.
july.narrative_hook: Lay-by in July was a word for white farmers — on the plantation, {ancestor_name} was put to clearing new ground, ditching, hauling, because the overseer had no concept of rest for people he did not consider fully human.

august.agricultural: Cotton bolls forming and early varieties beginning to open. Harvest preparation. The first picking could begin in late August on the earliest-maturing fields. Corn harvest beginning.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak — the most significant social and spiritual event of the year for both white and enslaved communities. Brush arbor revivals for the enslaved drew people from neighboring plantations — with or without the overseer's permission. Baptisms in the Alabama River or local creeks followed the revival.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest. Planters calculating expected yields. Cotton factors in Mobile preparing for the season's shipments.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 80-88F. Hurricane season. Intense thunderstorms. A late-summer hurricane or tropical storm could destroy the crop and flood the bottomlands.
august.narrative_hook: Revival in August was when {ancestor_name} heard something other than the overseer's voice — the preacher in the brush arbor offered a God who did not count tasks, and the singing carried across the dark prairie like the only free thing in the county.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking at full intensity. Every enslaved person in the fields — men, women, children. Picking quotas were strictly enforced: a skilled picker was expected to bring in 150-200 pounds per day; failure meant punishment. The work was backbreaking — stooping or crawling between rows, dragging a sack that grew heavier through the day, thorns on the cotton bolls cutting fingers. Picking continued from first light until dark.
september.social_civic: All social activity ceased. The harvest consumed everything. On Sundays, even the minimal rest was sometimes curtailed for the sake of the harvest.
september.economic: The first cotton reaching the gin. Income beginning. The harvest was the economic event that justified the entire year's investment in labor, land, and supplies.
september.weather_conditions: Hot but moderating — 76-84F. Rain could damage open bolls and delay picking. Every dry day was critical.
september.narrative_hook: September picking in the Black Belt was the cruelest month — {ancestor_name} in the rows from dawn to dark, 150 pounds the day's quota, the sack growing heavier and the bolls' thorns drawing blood, and the overseer weighing the sack at sunset.

october.agricultural: Cotton picking continuing at peak. Corn harvested and stored. Sweet potatoes dug. Hog fattening on mast in preparation for December slaughter.
october.social_civic: County court session. Limited social activity — the harvest dominated. On some plantations, enslaved workers were permitted to pick cotton on Saturday afternoons 'for themselves,' selling the small amounts to the planter at reduced rates.
october.economic: Primary income month. Cotton shipped to river landings and downriver to Mobile. Planters settling accounts with factors. The year's profit or loss becoming clear.
october.weather_conditions: Cooling — 62-72F. First frost possible by late October. Clear, dry weather ideal for picking.
october.narrative_hook: October was when the dark prairie gave up its cotton — {ancestor_name} picking in {county} County while the planters counted bales and calculated profits from labor that cost them nothing but the overseer's wages.

november.agricultural: Late cotton picking. Fields being cleaned. Winter preparation — firewood, fence repair, barn storage. Enslaved workers beginning maintenance tasks as the harvest wound down.
november.social_civic: Hunting season. Court session. The planter social season beginning again. For the enslaved, November brought the anxiety of sale season — planters settling debts sometimes sold enslaved workers in December and January.
november.economic: Final cotton sales. Planters settling accounts. The year's economic result determined. Debts paid or carried over.
november.weather_conditions: Cool — 48-58F. First hard frost by mid-November. Pleasant weather for outdoor work.
november.narrative_hook: November in {county} County brought the chill and the fear — {ancestor_name} knew that if the crop had not paid the planter's debts, December or January could bring the auction block.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event. Meat curing with salt and smoke. Fields dormant. Firewood cutting. Enslaved workers on maintenance tasks.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the most important break in the enslaved person's year. Most plantations allowed three to seven days of holiday. Enslaved families visited kin on neighboring plantations. Singing, dancing, and religious observance. The Christmas holiday was real but circumscribed — a gift of time from the enslaver that could be revoked. It was also the time when enslaved people most acutely felt the absence of sold family members.
december.economic: Year-end accounting. The domestic slave trade active — Selma's traders bought and sold through the winter. January hiring-out decisions being made. For the enslaved, the economic anxiety was not about debts but about bodies: whose would be sold, whose would be hired out, whose family would be broken.
december.weather_conditions: Cold for the Deep South — 42-52F. Rain common. Occasional frost.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in the quarters was the one week {ancestor_name} could rest — but the rest came with the knowledge that January might bring the hiring-out or the sale, and the week of grace was borrowed from a system that charged interest on everything, including time.

calendar_eras.description: After emancipation, the cotton calendar continued but the social architecture changed. Sharecropping replaced enslaved labor — the crop was the same, the soil was the same, and the poverty was structured rather than enforced by chains. The sharecropper family worked its own section of the plantation (typically 20-40 acres), made its own planting decisions within limits set by the landowner, and received one-third to one-half of the crop at settlement. But the crop-lien system — advances from the merchant or landowner at 30-50% annual interest, settled against the harvest — meant that most families ended the year in debt. The calendar was cotton's calendar, unchanged from the plantation era, but the experience was that of grinding poverty rather than chattel bondage. The boll weevil (arriving circa 1915) destroyed even this precarious economy.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Fence repair, wood cutting, hog killing (if not done in December). Preparing equipment for spring. Repairing the cabin — sharecropper housing was the same slave-quarter construction, rarely improved.
january.social_civic: Church services on Sunday — the Black church was the center of community life. New Year celebrations. Visiting among families. Contract negotiations with the landowner for the coming year — the sharecropper's annual moment of leverage, though the leverage was slight.
january.economic: The new year's credit line opened at the crossroads store or with the landowner. Interest on advances would run all year. The sharecropper signed (or marked) the crop-lien agreement — committing the coming harvest against the coming debt. For families who had cleared their previous year's debt (rare), January offered a brief moment of zero-balance freedom.
january.weather_conditions: Cool to cold — 42-50F. Rain frequent. Clay roads impassable after rain. The sharecropper's cabin — single-wall frame or log, no insulation — was cold.
january.narrative_hook: January was when {ancestor_name} signed the lien — marking an X on a contract that committed the family's labor to the landowner's arithmetic for another year, because the only alternative to debt was starvation.

february.agricultural: Late winter ground preparation. Breaking the heavy clay soil with a mule-drawn plow. Clearing any new ground assigned by the landowner. Seed preparation.
february.social_civic: Church. Limited visiting. School in session for Black children where Rosenwald schools existed — but school terms were short and tied to the agricultural calendar.
february.economic: Credit accumulating at the store. Seed and supply purchases. The sharecropper was already in debt before the first seed went in the ground.
february.weather_conditions: Cool and wet — 45-55F. Breaking ground in the clay was miserable work in wet weather.
february.narrative_hook: February in {county} County was when the mule and the clay began their argument — {ancestor_name} behind the plow, fighting soil that had grown cotton for fifty years and was giving back a little less every season.

march.agricultural: Cotton planting. Seeds dropped in furrows of the dark clay soil. Corn planted on the best bottomland. Garden plots behind the cabin planted with collards, sweet potatoes, beans, okra.
march.social_civic: Easter preparations. Church. School might end early as children were needed in the fields.
march.economic: Full credit extension. Every purchase at the store — seed, fertilizer, flour, fatback, cloth — was on credit at interest. The crop-lien arithmetic was already stacking against the family.
march.weather_conditions: Warming — 55-65F. Spring rains critical for germination. Heavy rain turned the clay roads to impassable mud.
march.narrative_hook: March planting in {county} County was an act of faith and arithmetic — {ancestor_name} put seed in the black clay knowing the merchant's interest rate would grow faster than the cotton, and praying the boll weevil would not finish what the debt had started.

april.agricultural: Cotton germinating. Replanting where stands were thin. First cultivation. Corn growing. Garden production beginning.
april.social_civic: Easter services. The church garden at some congregations was a communal effort.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 62-72F. Thunderstorms. Late frost risk minimal in the Black Belt.
april.narrative_hook: By April, the cotton was up and the year was committed — {ancestor_name} walked the rows in {county} County checking the stand, counting the gaps, and knowing that every empty spot was money the debt would still demand.

may.agricultural: Cotton chopping — hoeing between rows to control weeds. The entire family worked: men and women with hoes, older children helping. First cultivation with plow and sweep.
may.social_civic: School ended for Black children — the cotton demanded their labor. Church on Sunday was the only break.
may.economic: No income. Store credit accumulating. This was the lean season — the family survived on credit, garden vegetables, and whatever food stores remained from the previous year.
may.weather_conditions: Hot — 72-80F. Humidity climbing. Mosquitoes thick in the bottomlands.
may.narrative_hook: May was chopping month — {ancestor_name} with a hoe between the cotton rows, the whole family in the field, and the school closed because the cotton needed the children more than the children needed the school.

june.agricultural: Second and third cultivation. Cotton growing. Weeding continued. Corn tasseling. Garden producing heavily — okra, tomatoes, beans, squash.
june.social_civic: Church. Limited social activity. The heat and the fieldwork consumed everything.
june.economic: No income. Deepening credit. Some families supplemented by fishing in the Alabama River and its tributaries — catfish, bream, and turtle were important protein sources.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 78-86F. Thunderstorms. Malaria risk in the river bottoms.
june.narrative_hook: June in the Black Belt was when {ancestor_name} lived on what the garden gave and what the river offered — catfish from the Alabama River and collards from the patch behind the cabin, because the cotton money was months away and the store credit was all that stood between the family and hunger.

july.agricultural: Lay-by — the cotton growing on its own, requiring less intensive labor. This was the one genuine break in the sharecropper's calendar. Families repaired cabins, tended gardens, visited kin, and prepared for the harvest.
july.social_civic: July Fourth — observed with community gatherings, church picnics, and baseball games. Revival season beginning. Lay-by visiting — families traveled to see relatives in neighboring counties.
july.economic: No income. Store credit at maximum. Some men did day labor — chopping wood, hauling, odd jobs — for small cash payments.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 80-88F. Drought risk. The clay soil cracked in deep fissures during dry spells.
july.narrative_hook: Lay-by in July was when the cotton gave {ancestor_name} a week to breathe — the crop growing on its own, the hoe leaning against the cabin wall, and a Sunday afternoon at the church picnic where the Black Belt prairie felt almost like home instead of a sentence.

august.agricultural: Cotton bolls forming and early varieties opening. Harvest preparation. Late corn harvest. Sweet potato vines growing. The boll weevil (after ~1915) did its worst damage in August — boring into bolls and destroying the crop from within.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak — the most important religious and social event of the year. Evening services under brush arbors or in the church. Baptisms in the river. Courtship among young people. The revival was the social apex of the Black Belt's Black community.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest. Anxiety about the boll weevil (after 1915). The merchant and the landowner calculating expected yields.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 80-88F. Hurricane season. A tropical storm could destroy the crop.
august.narrative_hook: August revival in {county} County was sacred ground — {ancestor_name} heard preaching that named the suffering and promised something the cotton never had: grace that did not charge interest and mercy that did not require a settlement.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking at full intensity. The entire family in the fields — men, women, children. Picking was by hand, stooping between rows, dragging the cotton sack. A good picker might bring in 150-200 pounds per day. The sharecropper family picked its own section; the landowner did not directly supervise but the settlement was based on the crop brought to the gin.
september.social_civic: All social activity subordinated to the harvest. Children out of school (where school existed). Church attendance sparse — the harvest consumed every hour of daylight.
september.economic: First cotton reaching the gin. The sharecropper did not control the gin, the scale, or the grading — the landowner or the merchant determined what the cotton was worth. This asymmetry was the core of the system's exploitation.
september.weather_conditions: Hot but moderating — 76-84F. Dry weather essential for picking. Rain damaged open bolls.
september.narrative_hook: September picking in {county} County was the reckoning — {ancestor_name} in the rows from first light to last, the sack dragging heavier through the day, fingers bleeding from the boll thorns, and the knowledge that the gin scale and the merchant's arithmetic would determine whether the year ended in freedom or deeper debt.

october.agricultural: Cotton picking continuing. Corn gathered and stored. Sweet potatoes dug and banked. Hog fattening. The harvest pushed toward completion before frost.
october.social_civic: Court day at the county seat. Some community gatherings as the harvest wound down. Hunting season — squirrel, rabbit, possum, deer — critical protein for families whose diet was otherwise cornbread, fatback, and garden vegetables.
october.economic: Settlement approaching. Cotton ginned, graded, and sold. The sharecropper's share calculated against the year's advances. The annual reckoning — the moment that determined whether the family was free or trapped for another year.
october.weather_conditions: Cooling — 62-72F. First frost by late October. Clear skies for picking.
october.narrative_hook: October was when the year came to judgment — {ancestor_name} watching the cotton weighed at the gin in {county} County, the landowner's pencil figuring the share against the debt, and the answer almost always the same: not enough.

november.agricultural: Late cotton picking. Fields cleaned. Winter preparation — firewood, fence repair, cabin repair. Garden harvested and stored.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving observances. Hunting providing protein. Church homecomings. The brief window between harvest and winter when social life resumed.
november.economic: Settlement completed. Most families learned they were still in debt — the crop-lien interest had consumed whatever the cotton produced. Families who managed to clear their debt had a brief moment of economic freedom. Families in debt had no choice but to sign another year's lien.
november.weather_conditions: Cool — 48-58F. First hard frost. Pleasant weather for outdoor work.
november.narrative_hook: November was when {ancestor_name} learned the year's verdict — the settlement sheet at the store, the figures that never quite added up in the sharecropper's favor, and the landowner's offer to 'carry' the debt into next year, which was not generosity but bondage renewed.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event. Every part of the hog used: hams and shoulders salted and smoked, sausage ground, lard rendered, chitlins cleaned. Firewood cutting. Fields dormant.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount social event. Church services with singing that carried across the dark prairie. Family visiting. Gift-giving within whatever means the settlement allowed. Christmas was the one week when the cotton calendar released its grip. For children, it was the week of oranges, peppermint, and the possibility that the world was not entirely composed of cotton rows and debt.
december.economic: Year-end. The crop-lien cycle closed and reopened. Families in debt entered the new year already behind. Families considering migration — to Birmingham, to the North — made their decisions over Christmas, when the contrast between the Black Belt's poverty and the rumors of northern wages was sharpest.
december.weather_conditions: Cool to cold — 42-52F. Occasional frost. Rain common.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in {county} County was the one week {ancestor_name} could stop — hog killing done, cotton gone to market, and a few days when the family belonged to itself instead of to the crop. And for some families, Christmas was when the decision was made: stay and sign another lien, or follow the railroad north.

narrative_hooks: The cotton calendar owned the Black Belt — {ancestor_name} lived by its demands twelve months a year, and the only break was lay-by in July and Christmas in December, both borrowed from a system that charged interest on time itself.

Revival in August was the one event the cotton calendar could not consume — {ancestor_name} heard preaching that named the suffering and promised a reckoning that no settlement sheet at the store could prevent.

September picking was the cruelest arithmetic — {ancestor_name}'s fingers bled from the boll thorns, the sack grew heavier through the day, and the gin scale at the end would determine whether the year's labor produced freedom or deeper debt.

The settlement in November was the Black Belt's annual verdict — {ancestor_name} standing at the store while the merchant figured the account, knowing the interest had been growing as surely as the cotton, and the interest always won.

Christmas was the one week the cotton released its grip — {ancestor_name} could breathe, visit kin, sing in the church, and decide whether next year would begin with another lien or a train ticket north.

Hog killing in December was when the year's protein was secured — {ancestor_name} in the cold morning air, the hog scalded and scraped, every part saved because the Black Belt taught its people that waste was a luxury they had never been allowed.
source_references: Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Kolchin, Peter. First Freedom: The Responses of Alabama's Blacks to Emancipation and Reconstruction. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1972.
Rogers, William Warren, Robert David Ward, Leah Rawls Atkins, and Wayne Flynt. Alabama: The History of a Deep South State. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1994.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
Agee, James, and Walker Evans. Let Us Now Praise Famous Men. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1941.
